behaviors.description: Renamed files are categorized in rename section only, not counted as delete + addition
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is whether the tool invokes git with proper rename detection (e.g., `-M` flag) and correctly parses git's rename status in the output. This requires actual git commands on a repository with intentional renames to verify both the invocation and parsing logic work correctly together.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository with a committed file, then rename it in a new commit. Run the tool and verify: (1) rename category count is 1, (2) add/delete counts do not include the renamed file, (3) other categories remain unaffected.

behaviors.description: Binary files in diffs do not cause crashes and are counted in output
behaviors.justification: While parser robustness with 'Binary files differ' markers could theoretically be tested at unit level with mocked output, the real failure boundary includes git output format variations, file metadata handling for binary files, and encoding edge cases specific to actual binary objects. Only a real repository with binary files catches these interactions.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository containing binary files (.png, .bin, .pdf) committed alongside text files. Modify both binary and text files, run the tool, and verify: (1) tool exits without crashing, (2) binary files appear in modification count, (3) count is accurate and matches git log output.

behaviors.description: Empty repository with no commits produces empty report instead of error
behaviors.justification: Real failure is how actual git commands behave on a truly empty repository (no HEAD, no refs). Different git commands produce different results on empty repos (some fail, some return empty output). This is specific to real git behavior and cannot be mocked—the tool must be tested on an actual empty repository.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new git repository with no commits. Run the tool and verify: (1) tool exits with code 0 (success), (2) output is a valid empty report (either empty JSON object, empty list, or clearly marked 'no changes'), (3) no errors or exceptions are logged.

behaviors.description: Report counts exactly match the actual change history of the analyzed repository
behaviors.justification: Core end-to-end correctness test. While comprehensive unit tests with mocked git output could catch some counting logic errors, real failures include git version-specific output formats, merge commit handling, special character encodings in filenames, and interactions between git commands. Only testing against a known-good repository with controlled history can verify true accuracy.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository with a controlled commit history: commit 1 adds 3 files, commit 2 modifies 1 file, commit 3 deletes 1 file, commit 4 renames 1 file, and includes a merge commit. Run the tool and verify the reported counts of adds, deletes, modifies, and renames exactly match the git log.

behaviors.description: Output is identical whether analyzing staged changes, unstaged changes, or all changes
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary includes the tool's git command invocation logic and git's staging area behavior. Verifying true output identity requires manipulating git's staging area (git add, git reset) and invoking the tool in both states. Mocking git output for staged vs. unstaged scenarios cannot replicate git's actual behavior or catch subtle differences in the tool's state handling.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a real repository with both staged and unstaged changes. Run the tool once analyzing all changes, then run it again after unstaging (git reset) and re-staging in a different order. Verify the outputs are byte-for-byte identical in format and category counts.
